questionText: Disprove the statement: ``For all real numbers $a$ and $b$, $|a - b| = |a| - |b|$.''

steps.description: Read the claim as a universal statement.
steps.explanation: The claim asserts equality for *all* $a,b$; one pair where the sides differ disproves it.
We need a single pair making the two sides unequal.

steps.description: Note the right side can be negative.
steps.explanation: An absolute value $|a-b|$ is never negative, yet $|a|-|b|$ can be, so pick $|b|>|a|$.
Here $|b|=2>1=|a|$, so the right side should turn negative.
The left side is $|-1|=1$, a non-negative number.
The right side is $1-2=-1$, which is negative.
At $a=1,\ b=2$ the sides are $1$ and $-1$, so the claim is disproved.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Take $a=1,\ b=2$: then $|a-b| = 1$ but $|a|-|b| = -1$. Since $1 \neq -1$, the statement is false. $\blacksquare$
